topic: A person finds out their article was published under someone else's name
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox like a physical blow. My hands, already clammy, started to tremble so violently I had to grip the edge of my desk to steady them. The subject line was innocuous, but the body of the message read like a betrayal. My meticulously researched piece, the one I poured months of sleepless nights into, was credited to a complete stranger. A stranger with a different name, a different portfolio. I reread the text, my eyes darting back and forth across the screen, searching for a typo, a misplaced comma, anything to suggest this was a mistake. But the attribution was solid, undeniable. A cold dread began to coil in my stomach, tightening with every second. I stood up abruptly, pacing the small confines of my apartment, the floorboards seeming to amplify the frantic drumming in my chest. Who was this person? What had they done? I needed to call someone, to demand an explanation, but the words caught in my throat, leaving only a dry, rasping sound.

[story 2]

I stared at the screen, the fluorescent office light buzzing overhead suddenly feeling intensely bright and hostile. My breath hitched, shallow and quick, as I reread the publication details. The name attached to my breakthrough analysis—my magnum opus—belonged to someone else entirely. A wave of nausea washed over me, making my vision swim slightly at the edges. I pressed the heels of my palms against my eyes, trying to physically block out the words. My jaw clenched so hard I could feel the strain in my molars. Every instinct screamed at me to flee, to slam my laptop shut and disappear, but my feet remained rooted to the carpet. I began to rapidly type, drafting an email, but my fingers kept slipping, the keys feeling foreign and unresponsive. A frantic energy surged through me, a desperate need to fix this rupture in reality, but the sheer enormity of the situation rendered me utterly immobile.

[story 3]

When the notification popped up, the world seemed to tilt on its axis. I blinked several times, trying to force the information into a coherent shape, but it remained fractured and horrifying. It was my work, the piece that had been praised, reviewed, and lauded, yet the byline belonged to someone else. A sharp intake of breath escaped my lungs, followed by a sudden, intense cold spreading from my core outward. I dropped my coffee mug; the ceramic shattered on the floor, the sound swallowed instantly by the sudden, roaring silence in my ears. I knelt down, my gaze locked on the offending text, my hands shaking uncontrollably as I reached out to touch the screen, as if the very words were contaminated. A high-pitched whine started building behind my eyes, a sensation that made focusing on anything else impossible. I needed answers, desperately, but my throat constricted, making any attempt at speech feel like trying to push through thick mud.
